question: An <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> particle and a carbon <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn></math> atom has same kinetic energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi></math>. The ratio of their de-Broglie wavelengths <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>α</mi></msub><mo>:</mo><msub><mi>λ</mi><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>12</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is
solution:
De-Broglie wavelength is stated as λ=hp=h2mK, where, K is kinetic energy and m is mass.

Here, Kα=KC‐12.

Now, the ratio λαλC12=12m4m=3:1

subject: Physics
q: Proton <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mo>)</mo></math> and electron <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mo>)</mo></math> will have same de-Broglie wavelength when the ratio of their momentum is (assume, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>p</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1849</mn></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math>)
question: Proton <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mo>)</mo></math> and electron <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mo>)</mo></math> will have same de-Broglie wavelength when the ratio of their momentum is (assume, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>p</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1849</mn></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math>)
solution:
The De Broglie wavelength is λ=hmv

The momentum is given by p=mv

The momentum of proton is pp=mpvp

The momentum of electron is  p'=meve

Since h is the Planck's constant and it is given that both wavelengths are same, i.e.

λp=λe, so pp=p'.

q:
Paragraph: Wave property of electrons implies that they will show diffraction effects. Davisson and Germer demonstrated this by diffracting electrons from crystals. The law governing the diffraction from a crystal is obtained by requiring that electron waves reflected from the planes of atoms in a crystal interfere constructively (see in figure).
Question: In an experiment, electrons are made to pass through a narrow slit of width 'd' comparable to their de Broglie wavelength. They are detected on a screen at a distance ' $D$ ' from the slit (see figure).
Which of the following graph can be expected to represent the number of electrons ' $N$ ' detected as a function of the detector position ' $y$ ' $(y=0$ corresponds to the middle of the slit)?
question:
Paragraph: Wave property of electrons implies that they will show diffraction effects. Davisson and Germer demonstrated this by diffracting electrons from crystals. The law governing the diffraction from a crystal is obtained by requiring that electron waves reflected from the planes of atoms in a crystal interfere constructively (see in figure).
Question: In an experiment, electrons are made to pass through a narrow slit of width 'd' comparable to their de Broglie wavelength. They are detected on a screen at a distance ' $D$ ' from the slit (see figure).
Which of the following graph can be expected to represent the number of electrons ' $N$ ' detected as a function of the detector position ' $y$ ' $(y=0$ corresponds to the middle of the slit)?
solution: Diffraction pattern will be wider than the slit.

subject: Physics
q:
With reference to the observations in photo-electric effect, identify the correct statements from below:

A. The square of maximum velocity of photoelectrons varies linearly with frequency of incident light.

B. The value of saturation current increases on moving the source of light away from the metal surface.

C. The maximum kinetic energy of photo-electrons decreases on decreasing the power of LED (light emitting diode) source of light.

D. The immediate emission of photo-electrons out of metal surface can not be explained by particle nature of light/electromagnetic waves.
E. Existence of threshold wavelength can not be explained by wave nature of light/electromagnetic waves.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
With reference to the observations in photo-electric effect, identify the correct statements from below:

A. The square of maximum velocity of photoelectrons varies linearly with frequency of incident light.

B. The value of saturation current increases on moving the source of light away from the metal surface.

C. The maximum kinetic energy of photo-electrons decreases on decreasing the power of LED (light emitting diode) source of light.

D. The immediate emission of photo-electrons out of metal surface can not be explained by particle nature of light/electromagnetic waves.
E. Existence of threshold wavelength can not be explained by wave nature of light/electromagnetic waves.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
From Einstein's photoelectric equation, 

Maximum kinetic energy, KEmax=12mvmax2=hf-ϕ, where, vmax is maximum velocity of photoelectrons, h is Planck's constant, f is frequency of incident light and ϕ is work function of metal.

From above relation, we can see that the square of maximum velocity of photoelectrons varies linearly with frequency of incident light.

If we increase the intensity of the incident radiation by keeping the frequency constant, then the value of saturation current increases in proportion to the intensity of the incident radiation. Thus, saturation current decreases on moving the source of light away from the metal surface.

The kinetic energy of the photoelectrons is directly proportional to the light frequency, and it remains constant as light amplitude or power of source increases.

Photoelectric effect cannot be explained on the basis of wave nature because experimentally a particular energy called the work function of the metal surface in needed to eject electrons from surface, irrespective of the time for which light is incident. 

Photoelectric effect can be explained by particle nature of light (i.e. quantum theory). Threshold wavelength is maximum wavelength at which emission takes place.

solution:
From Einstein’s photoelectric equation,
K.Emax=hcλ-hcλ0 (here, λ0= threshold wavelength)
=1237260-1237380
=1237380-260380×260
=1.5 eV

question:
The magnetic field associated with a light wave is given, at the origin, by B=B0 sin⁡3.14×107ct+sin⁡6.28×107ct. If this light falls on a silver plate having a work function of 4.7eV, what will be the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons?

(c=3×108 m s−1,h=6.6×10−34 J s)
solution:
B=B0sin⁡3.14×107ct+sin⁡6.28×107ct

Maximum KE will associate with the maximum frequency

fmax⁡ =6.28×107c2π=107×3×108 Hz           =3×1015 Hz

KEmax=hfmax-ϕ

=6.63×10-34×3×10151.6×10-19-4.7 eV

=7.72 eV

subject: Physics
q: Two sources of light emit with a power of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math>. The ratio of number of photons of visible light emitted by each source having wavelengths <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> respectively, will be :
question: Two sources of light emit with a power of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math>. The ratio of number of photons of visible light emitted by each source having wavelengths <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> respectively, will be :
solution:
Let n1 and n2 be the number of photons coming out from the first and the second source of light respectively per second.

So, for the first source,

n1×hcλ1=200   ...1

And, for the second source,

n2×hcλ2=200   ...2

From equations (1) and (2), it implies that

n1n2=λ1λ2=300500⇒n1n2=35

q: If \(\lambda\) and \(K\) are de Broglie wavelength and kinetic energy, respectively, of a particle with constant mass. The correct graphical representation for the particle will be
question: If \(\lambda\) and \(K\) are de Broglie wavelength and kinetic energy, respectively, of a particle with constant mass. The correct graphical representation for the particle will be
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mK}}} \\
& \lambda^2=\frac{\mathrm{h}^2}{2 \mathrm{~m}}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{k}}\right) \\
& \mathrm{Y}=\mathrm{cx}^2
\end{aligned}$

Upward facing parabola passing through origin.

question:

options: Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is the correct explanation of Statement-1.
Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is not the correct explanation of Statement-1.
Statement-1 is false, Statement-2 is true.
Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is false.

question: A small object at rest, absorbs a light pulse of power <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mW</mi></math> and duration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>ns</mi></math>. Assuming speed of light as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The momentum of the object becomes equal to :
solution:
Momentum p=EnergyESpeed of lightc

=Power×timec

=20×10-3W300×10-9s3×108 m s-1

=2×10-17 kg m s-1

As momentum is conserved, therefore momentum of object will be 2×10-17 kg m s-1.

question: An electron with speed $v$ and a photon with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> have the same de-Broglie wavelength. If the kinetic energy and momentum of electron are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> and that of photon are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mrow><mi>p</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mrow><mi>p</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow></msub></math> respectively. Which of the following is correct?
solution:
We have, λp=hpp and λe=hpe

Given here, λe=λp, then pp=pe

Ratio of their energy is EphEe=hcλPpe22m=2mcpe=2mcmv=2cv

subject: Physics
q: The de-Broglie wavelength of a particle having kinetic energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi><mo>.</mo></math> How much extra energy must be given to this particle so that the de-Broglie wavelength reduces to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>75</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the initial value?
question: The de-Broglie wavelength of a particle having kinetic energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi><mo>.</mo></math> How much extra energy must be given to this particle so that the de-Broglie wavelength reduces to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>75</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the initial value?
solution:
Recall the formula of de-Broglie wavelength, λ=hp, here p is the linear momentum of the particle, now use the relation between linear momentum and kinetic energy, p=2Em⇒E∝1λ2⇒E=kλ2

and E'=16k9λ2⇒ΔE=7E9.

subject: Physics
q: The graph shows variation of stopping potential $V_o$ with the frequency $\nu$ of the incident radiation for three photosensitive metals $X_1$, $X_2$ and $X_3$. Which metal will give out electrons with greater kinetic energy, for the same wavelength of incident radiation?
question: The graph shows variation of stopping potential $V_o$ with the frequency $\nu$ of the incident radiation for three photosensitive metals $X_1$, $X_2$ and $X_3$. Which metal will give out electrons with greater kinetic energy, for the same wavelength of incident radiation?
options: All the metals will give out photo electrons with same kinetic energies.
solution:
From Einstein's photoelectric equation, the maximum kinetic energy of emitted photoelectrons is given by:

$K_{ ext{max}} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} - \Phi$

where $\lambda$ is the wavelength of incident radiation and $\Phi$ is the work function of the metal.

The work function is related to the threshold frequency $ u_0$ by $\Phi = h u_0$. From the given graph, the x-intercept represents the threshold frequency. We can observe that:

$ u_0(X_1) < u_0(X_2) < u_0(X_3)$

Therefore, the work functions of the metals follow the order:

$\Phi(X_1) < \Phi(X_2) < \Phi(X_3)$

For the same wavelength $\lambda$ of incident radiation, the term $\dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ is constant. Thus, the kinetic energy $K_{ ext{max}}$ will be greatest for the metal with the smallest work function.

Since metal $X_1$ has the minimum work function, it will emit electrons with the greatest kinetic energy.

Answer: $X_1$

2=mempve4ve
∴ mp=me8

E→=10-3cos2πx5×10-7-2π×6×1014tx^ N/c
k=2π5×10-7
And ω=6×1014×2π ⇒f=6×1014 Hz
E=ϕ+KEmax
KEmax=hf-ϕ
=6.6×10-34×6×10141.6×10-19-2
=2.475-2=0.48 eV
∴ Stopping potential is 0.48 V

options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mi>v</mi><mn mathvariant="italic">1</mn><mn mathvariant="italic">2</mn></msubsup><mo mathvariant="italic">-</mo><msubsup><mi>v</mi><mn mathvariant="italic">2</mn><mn mathvariant="italic">2</mn></msubsup><mo mathvariant="italic">=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn mathvariant="italic">2</mn><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mi mathvariant="italic">h</mi></mrow><mrow><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mi mathvariant="italic">m</mi></mrow></mfrac><mstyle mathvariant="italic"><mrow><mo>[</mo><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>]</mo></mrow></mstyle></math>
solution:
12mv12=hf1-ϕ

12mv22=hf2-ϕ

v12-v22=2h mf1-f2

subject: Physics
q:
If the deBroglie wavelength of an electron is equal to $10^{-}$ times the wavelength of a photon of frequency $6 \times 10^{14}$ $\mathrm{Hz}$, then the speed of electron is equal to :

(Speed of light $\left.=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\right)$

Planck's constant $=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J} . \mathrm{s}$

Mass of electron $\left.=9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}\right)$

subject: Physics
q: A proton, a neutron, an electron and an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math>-particle have same energy. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>p</mi></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>n</mi></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>α</mi></msub></math> are the de Broglie's wavelengths of proton, neutron, electron and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> particle respectively, then choose the correct relation from the following
question: A proton, a neutron, an electron and an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math>-particle have same energy. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>p</mi></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>n</mi></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>α</mi></msub></math> are the de Broglie's wavelengths of proton, neutron, electron and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> particle respectively, then choose the correct relation from the following
solution:
The de-Broglie wavelength λ = h2m(K.E.)

where, h and K.E. are constant

λ∝1m

m= Mass of the particle

Thus, with increasing mass of particle de-Broglie wavelength decreases.

Order of mass me<mp<mn<mα

Order of de-Broglie wavelength is λa<λn<λp<λe

It falls normally on a metal (work function <math><mi>φ</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>e</mi><mi>V</mi></math> ) of surface area of <math><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> . If one in <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> photons ejects an election, total number of electrons ejected in <math><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup></math> . <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>h</mi><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1240</mn><mi></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1.6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>19</mn></mrow></msup><mi>J</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi></mi></math> then <math><mi>x</mi></math> is ___________
solution:
Energy of photon. E=1240310 nm=4eV>2eV (So photoelectric effect will take place)
=4×1.6×10-19=6.4×10-19joule
Number of photons falling per second
=6.4×10-5×16.4×10-19=1014
Number of photoelectron emitted per second
=1014103=1011

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A): The electromagnetic wave exerts pressure on the surface on which they are allowed to fall.
Reason (R): There is no mass associated with the electromagnetic waves.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A): The electromagnetic wave exerts pressure on the surface on which they are allowed to fall.
Reason (R): There is no mass associated with the electromagnetic waves.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
solution:
Assertion (A) is true because electromagnetic waves carry energy and momentum. When they strike a surface, they transfer momentum to it, which results in a force and consequently exerts radiation pressure on the surface.

Reason (R) is true because electromagnetic waves consist of photons, which have zero rest mass. Thus, there is no rest mass associated with electromagnetic waves.

However, the pressure exerted by electromagnetic waves is due to the momentum they carry ($p = \dfrac{E}{c}$), not because they are massless. Therefore, both (A) and (R) are true, but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A).

Answer: Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)

subject: Physics
q: Consider an electron in a hydrogen atom, revolving in its second excited state (having radius <math><mn>4.65</mn><mi> </mi><mtext>Å</mtext></math> ). The de-Broglie wavelength of this electron is:
question: Consider an electron in a hydrogen atom, revolving in its second excited state (having radius <math><mn>4.65</mn><mi> </mi><mtext>Å</mtext></math> ). The de-Broglie wavelength of this electron is:
solution:
According to Bohr’s atomic model
mvr=nh2π
And the de-Broglie wavelength
λ=hmv
From the above two equations we can write, λ=2πrn
In the 2nd excited state
λ=2πr3
∴  λ=23π×4.65  Å=9.7 Å

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : The photoelectric effect does not take place, if the energy of the incident radiation is less than the work function of a metal.

Reason R : Kinetic energy of the photoelectrons is zero, if the energy of the incident radiation is equal to the work function of a metal.
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : The photoelectric effect does not take place, if the energy of the incident radiation is less than the work function of a metal.

Reason R : Kinetic energy of the photoelectrons is zero, if the energy of the incident radiation is equal to the work function of a metal.
options: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is not correct
A is not correct but R is correct
solution:
Work function of a metal is defined as the minimum amount of energy needed for ejection of photoelectron. Therefore, Assertion is correct.

Incident energy may or may not be completely absorbed by the photoelectron. Therefore, kinetic energy of a photoelectron may be zero even if the incident radiation has energy greater than work function. Hence, though the reason is correct, it is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
